commentBody:
maybe first commentor? but only because the link isn't working and I had to get here the hard way.
enjoyed the puzzle.

A great puzzle. The puns brought me back to the first NYT puzzles I attempted. 
It’s feeling like Spring here!
A bit easier than usual for Sunday puzzles, but enjoyable nonetheless. Thank you.

My opinion is that this is a fun, funny puzzle with a lot of puns that Andrzej will hate.

In other words, a perfect Sunday.

“New York paper’s crosswords aren’t what they used to be!”?
TIMES, THEY ARE A CHANGIN,
Maybe, but I have no complaints today. 
I appreciate the difficulty of finding and placing all these themes and I’m glad you stuck with it, Sam. These all made me laugh inside and finding a place for 8 long themers is no walk in the park. I ESPECIALLY appreciate the time Sam and/or the editors took to polish some of the shorter clues—the clues for ANT, WATER, ZIT, NOMAD, and TATAS show that just because an entry has been around the block a few times doesn’t mean you can’t take time to find a fresh clue for it.
I found this entertaining and reasonably challenging for a Sunday. 
Last comment—one can’t see EJECT in a puzzle, especially clued the way it was, without saying a prayer for the safe return of both our downed pilots in Iraq. God speed!
This was my favorite solve in a long time -- so fun and so funny.  I laughed out loud as I solved 2D, about 2/3 of my way through the puzzle.
I loved the theme and the long solves. Very enjoyable all around!
Punny crosswords are my favorite crosswords
… but are we going to get the SNARFS ScARFS controversy again?
Might I suggest that, for some of us, the product names were not well known. But somehow deductible from crosses.

No, the constructor is not that Gary Larson.

Lots of Sunday fun for USians. International solvers may not be familiar with all of the brands in the themers. Nice fill and enjoyable clues, too.

(Way to) Play it, Sam.
My Diary of a Crossword Fiend review: https://crosswordfiend.com/2026/04/04/sunday-april-5-2026/#ny
I have to admit, i puzzled for a moment over “Don’t touch that Dove??”  A very clever puzzle overall.  One of my faster Sunday times.

“This is the perfect time to change to another bug spray!”
TRADE IN THE OFF SEASON
“Our deodorant expires very quickly”
YOU CAN’T KEEP A SECRET
“Our laundry detergent is no longer very popular”
TIDE IS GOING OUT
“Our dish soap formula no longer works”
DAWN IS BREAKING

These just go to show how hard this is, all of the included ones are so much better!

Tough Sunday today, but a fun rewarding theme.  Figuring out the cliche saying that has a brand name in it.

A couple areas were pretty tough for me, like REIKI/ZORBA, huge Natick for me.  I also didn't quite get the ZIT clue so my last square was the Z, I cycled through all the letters then was surprised when the Z set off the victory jingle.  Also STOGIE/OGEE.

The first time I can remember where there was a clue that could equally be SCARFS or SNARFS and it actually turned out to be SCARFS.

A fun puzzle.  I got it filled in, but no happy music. So I went to the Wordplay and learned the actress is "BRAGA", not 'Brala'.  'Brala' came about with the crossing word of 'Looniness'.  With that fix, I then had 'Gooniness'.  But still no happy music.  So I brought up the answer pdf and found myself returning to first error:  the word is GOOFINESS!

Thank you for the fun challenge!  Happy Easter if you celebrate.  Happy Sunday whatever you do!

Loved the puns. UNITED WE STAND made me giggle out loud.

Also- US AGAINST THE WORLD was strangely prescient.

Literal winnowing has nothing at all in common with literal paring, although I can imagine metaphorical usage in which one can substitute for the other; Alice Braga and Clarence Clemons were unknowns; and "I'm up"? Do people say that, outside of softball? All that made for a tough quadrant!

***

As happenstance should have it, earlier this afternoon, the Wikipedia Effect took me to the entry on "Wheel of Fortune." From that article (slightly edited):

"After Stafford left to become a humanitarian worker,[41] over two hundred applicants signed up for a nationwide search to be her replacement.[55] Griffin eventually winnowed/pared the list to three finalists, which consisted of Summer Bartholomew, former Playboy centerfold Vicki McCarty, and Vanna White.[56] Griffin gave each of the three women an opportunity to win the job by putting them in a rotation for several weeks after Stafford's departure.[8] In December 1982, Griffin named White as Stafford's successor,[57] saying that he felt she was capable of activating the puzzle board letters (which is the primary role of the Wheel co-host) better than anyone else who had auditioned."

***

Happy Easter to all who celebrate!

What a coincidence on the timing of 41D...

Also, again when coming from the Games app, I couldn't click on the little comment bubble in the lower right corner of the Wordplay column to get to the comments. I wish they'd fix that.

Adding to the chorus of voices saying the puzzles are getting easier, I submit that I have set personal bests twice this week - including today.
I was also within a minute of another PB.

It's making me feel smarter than I suspect I really am.

Given the theme, it would have been fun to include the “big name in” trope somewhere else in the puzzle. Those crack me up for some reason.
“Big name in powdered milk”.
“Big name in ball peen hammers”.
“Big name in alpaca socks”.

Enjoyable puzzle and thanks for all the hard work, Sam! Loved 23A and 115A. I print it out and don't time myself but I got stuck at the intersection of 6A and 6D. No idea on either count, so I looked them up. I hate when that happens. So, well, now I know.
57A was the best, but 115A is also pretty good. Fun puzzle!
Anyone else unable to access the comments link via the Android app? I have to search for the Wordplay Column on Google and get to it through there ...
I did not like this puzzle, especially for a Monday.
loved the themers!
81D is quite prescient given the fighter planes that got shot down over Iran

Apropos of Artemis: my all-time favorite NYT crossword clue--

[Cosmic eruption on the moon?]

CHEESEQUAKE

This was a classic Sunday puzzle with a very clever theme.  My favorites were JUSTTAKEAGUESS and DONTTOUCHTHATDIAL.  I fear the heavy focus on US brands and idioms may frustrate some of our non-US colleagues.
Theme was pretty silly but in the best possible way. Lots of fun.
I haven't finished the crossword.  I finished the midi don't get "as literally seen in this clue?"  There's a theme, but i don't get it.

Surprised to see xwordstats label this "Hard"

I enjoyed it, but flew thru it with very little resistance.
Solved it, but still don't get the DigestIf answer.

Enjoyed it muchly. Not American, but was able to deduce the brands. That being said “UNITED WE STAND” is global and funny. 6A/D was worked out by running the alphabet, but felt the crossword was fair. Loved the conjoint at 77A/D of CLEMSON/CLEMENS. In honour of which … https://youtu.be/iMq5XRGWh_E?is=HhhXSrx8loLVBL5U
Loved this, Dr. Donaldson

I'll admit that I didn't get them all, but this was a very refreshing puzzle. The viewpoint was delightfully skewed and so much fun to figure out. NB: The oreos are back. Second day in a row. Will the emus follow on?

My Philippine friend says no one says ADOBOS -- it's "adobo" or "adobo marinades" but doesn't get pluralized. Like "sheep" or "deer" or whatnot. 

(I was amused at ADOBOS being right over MANILA. Too bad the pattern didn't continue...)

Also, I am never going to remember SNERT.
Clever and fun!! I was lucky to have a class with Professor Donaldson in the Tax LLM program at the University of Washington! Nice job!

Not my puzzle at all. Brand-based puns? I can hardly imagine a theme I would enjoy less, as a pun and brand hater.

The fill didn't vibe with me, either.

I abandoned the solve when I wasn't even half-way done. It feels great to not be a slave to the streak.

Loved it!!!!! Funny, fun, bright and cheery. 'Don't touch that dial!' Nice.

Hope we see this constructor again soon!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: Another cutesy, mostly fill-in-the-blanks Sunday typing exercise.

Just like last Sunday, I got cheated.  I completed the puzzle but got neither the "Congratulations" or the "You missed at least one letter" message.

Where is the "puzzle page" that I am supposed to "scroll down to the bottom of" to find the tab that says "Contact us" in order to "get help with a technical issue"????  I asked you this question last week, too, but never got an answer.
"United we stand" sounds like the airline's future Ultra Basic Economy class.

I got a lot of the themed entries pretty quickly but I'm embarrassed how long some of the fill took me. The cluing brought several "oohhh" moments - all in all, a fun Sunday. 
Happy Easter to those who celebrate!
Delightful!

[Are you sure we're not double-billing you?]

KEEPYOUREYESONTHE TARGET

Even exhausted and sore 
from the endless abyss
of travel lines,
TSA, passports, 
planes, trains, trams and automobiles,

This was fun.
Doggone good puzzle. 

I just want to be home…

Fun theme today. I enjoyed the clever reinterpretations of the theme answers. Found the puzzle not too challenging. I had SNERd before SNERT, but realized pretty quickly that dEMPURA isn’t a fried Japanese fare. 
Regarding 80 across, I and my son have both had 20 year (non overlapping) stints working at the fruit company and I have yet to have one of their products crumble on me. 
Some regular readers here may be interested in hearing that my mother Suejean, who used to be a regular commentator here, has moved to a rest home in California after 48 years living in Yorkshire, England. She still does the occasional NY Times crossword, but is no longer up to the task of contributing to this forum. She is happy and enjoys visits from her children, grandchildren, and great grandchildren who all live nearby.
What fun a puzzle!  The theme entries made me smile.
I needed quite a few crosses to guess the themers, and each one was appropriately groan worthy.  Fun solve!

Thank you for your time and creativity, Professor Donaldson.Well worth the effort. Great fun and challenging. Loved the themed clues as I figured each one out. First and favorite was "Don't Touch That Dial!"

I am a Filipino American from San Diego so the first few answers already hooked me in! 

I wanted to share that one of my faavorite memories as a child was packing a balikbayan box, or a big box of wares to send back to PH for our family there. The main staples, other than clothes, were chocolates, lotions, and boxes of DIAL!

Thank you for sparking this memory.
Loved it! It reminded me of the puzzles of the great Merl Reagle.

After uncovering a couple of these theme answers, I found that I couldn’t wait to uncover more.  

That doesn’t happen very often with me.  Much of the time, after uncovering a couple of punny theme answers, I become involved in guessing each of the others as mainly a riddle-cracking exercise.  

That is enjoyable in its own right, but when you add that extra layer of being so entertained by the answers that you can’t wait to see more, and you feel, while solving, like a kid on an Easter egg hunt, well, that is special.   

It helped that the first one I got was DON’T TOUCH THAT DIAL, where its moxie and humor, combined with the cleverness of the theme gimmick itself, practically made me stand up and applaud.  I eagerly bounded on the hunt for more.

I stayed on that what-else-is-he-going-to-come-up-with pursuit through to the end, and what a rare and wonderful way to experience a crossword, especially a Sunday.

All the time and effort over those months that you put into this, Samuel, handsomely paid off for me, and surely many others.  I loved this.  Thank you!

Did CLEMONS go to CLEMSON? He did here!

This was a fun puzzle with a cute theme. Thanks for sticking with it, Mr. Donaldson! You made my morning.

Obligatory not USian, but oddly enough knew that Dial was soap but had never heard of the phrase “Don’t Touch That Dial”. Is it some variation of “Don’t Fix What Ain’t Broke”, perhaps?
All in all a very enjoyable and breezy Sunday - looking forward to more like it.

Thoroughly enjoyed this, despite being hampered by the unknown brand names etc. it was the UNITED… phrase that let me in. I deduce that the airline is akin to Ryanair in perhaps less than stellar customer service. 
As for 34D. Oof. Not so punny anymore.
Fwiw, United We Stand might be a thing of the present, and not the future. Check this out: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OvHVen3TrXU. Pretty cool when life imitates crossword puzzles. tc
I had to give up and look at the answer key to discover my error at the intersection of 7D and 27A.  Darla looked pretty plausible, and I figured LNN must be a courier brand.
Cute.

This was fun. Loved the CLEMSON/CLEMONS pairing.

What almost killed me was PARE/BRAGA. Never heard of the person (is it an actor or a character?) or the show, so I was at a disadvantage.

Had nilNIL before ONENIL; as a non-fan I will often snidely remark that one of the sides has an insurmountable ONE NIL lead. 

Thanks!

“Don’t remain blind to the potential harms from our products and data farms.”

OPEN A I

Once the puzzle is solved, I often wonder about the construction process. In this case Sam Donaldson’s notes give us a sense of the thought and effort that may go into a clever theme and very entertaining Sunday. Solving today at 5am, even the iffy answers made me smile. (Um, BAAING anyone?) Thanks, Sam. I loved this one.

[Once you start reading our magazine, it's impossible to stop!]
-
-
-
-
-
TIMESUCK

A  nice and fairly easy puzzle, but this one didn't get me all roiled up by being *too* easy. That's because there were lots of fun theme answers, my favorite being, as others have commented DONT TOUCH THAT DIAL, UNITED WE STAND, and NO PAIN, NO GAIN. Also some cute misdirects. I fully expected that our non-American solvers would be outraged by this. so I was pleasantly surprised that only our friend Andrejz, with whom I'm usually in lockstep with, seemed to be one of the very few.

The answers that tripped me up for a bit were BRAGA, Never heard of her, only Sonia. Also has OSSEOUS  and OSTEAL (though that didn't fit) before OSTEOID. And CLEMONS was unknown. But they all worked out in the crosses, and I enjoyed today's Sunday puzzle for the first time in a long time.

Having read all the comments to date, I was surprised that no one commented on not being able to understand the answer BARREL for the clue "Monkey's place, maybe". It came easy to me, but it seems very dated, which I am!

Thanks for a fun puzzle, Sam.

Not my best effort! Even after revealing all the themes (what does USA GAINS THE WORLD refer to?) I could only solve about half. But revealed the, to me, trivia in case it comes up again.

Dirt pie - or Ultra Processed Pie, it seems. 

A clue I couldn't understand was Q3 starter/Jul. Help appreciated.

The first comments explaining "Don't touch that dial" cited television; an "older" poster had to step in to point out that the term dates from radio days. I wonder how many younger listeners grasp the meaning of this oldie: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Bb85NvjbBm8

The theme and thematic entries were cute, and a couple made me smile. And I loved the CLEMSON/CLEMONS pairing in the SE.

Some of the fill---well we have ENTERON, ITISON and SATON in the grid. 

OSTEOID and BIENNIA are unusual words; I don't think I've come across either in anything I've read. While I can imagine the former in use, it seems more difficult to concoct an actual need for the latter.

All I can say is thank goodness that my brain eventually came up with ADOBOS. I think I vaguely knew AMF---can't recall its meaning or connection to bowling---but DARIA as clued was a total unknown. Would have guessed MARIA for sure.

FUTURAMA and GIJANE also popped into my head from somewhere, but BERTIE and BRAGA required the crosses.

All in all, I liked this one. It felt challenging for a Sunday, though I got it solved without help in just over thirty minutes.

If the choice is between tide pods and a product to remove dust, I’d choose the latter.


Take the pledge

(I agree with Mr. Donaldson. These are really hard to come up with)
Loved the theming when I discovered it, but Snert made my Sunday morning. And that's no lie.

“Our cereals are sugar bombs, and result in a complete glucose crash after the buzz.”

POST: APOCALYPTIC
PUZZLES RIDDLED WITH POLITICAL BIAS

This should have been run on Father's Day for all the dad jokes! 

So much fun. Thank you!

Do I really have to watch "The Office" and listen to Ariana Grande?  Tune in to cartoon sci-fi? Take statistics?   REALLY?

I finally got enough crosses to get one of the themers, and I realized the brand names would be unaltered; that helped a bit...but only a bit.  Tough but fair--great Sunday grid!

Favorite thought:  40A.
I can't tell you how many "Good Ole Days" rants I endure in relative silence...although the steam coming out of my ears might have given me away.  My chief fear, nowadays, is a return to those days of suppression, oppression, and depression for many.  

Well, everyone  --enjoy a peaceful Sunday.  (It's very windy, gray, wet, and chilly here, after some days in the 80's.  Practice for being in Seattle?)

I will continue to "advertise" a Sunday (April 26th) Meet-up for us Puzzlers at Cloud City Coffee in the "Maple Leaf" neighborhood.  It looks like early afternoon will suit the most folks...  All who can show up it will be greeted with joy.  I will be disguised as a mid-sized old lady with white hair, armed with a FriXion pen.

Typical long workout for me, but a really enjoyable puzzle. All the theme answers somewhat in the vicinity of dad jokes so should have been right up my alley (oops). And... appropriate puzzle find today. A Sunday from September 11 (!), 1983 by Alfio Micci with the title: "Sins of Omission." Some theme clue and answer examples: "A for sore eyes." SIGHTUNSEEN "A in time..." DROPSTITCH "upon a time..." ONCEREMOVED "A for all seasons." INVISIBLEMAN And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=9/11/1983&g=27&d=A ...

My CW skill of thinking in multiple words needs lots of polishing. Trying to think of a single word for Impersonates ultimately required the Autocheck. That SE corner was difficult for me. Very fun Sunday. Thank you, creator. Cheers.

(One more, sorry)

“Other cookie companies may reveal their ingredients, but in our case, you really don’t want to know, so we lie!”

TRUTHORDARE

An excellent Sunday puzzle.
Good theme, well executed.
Appropriate level of difficulty. Good fill.
As the executives say in Hollywood, "No Nits!"
...or something like that.

Thanks, Samuel Donaldson.  Your hard work paid off, and I'm glad to be the beneficiary.

The caption to today's Wordplay photo:
"Our extra large printers provide home security!"

I assume that the editors are giving us a bonus themer.  Has anyone figured it out?
Something involving (ink)jets, perhaps?

OH! I think I've got it:
BIG BROTHER IS WATCHING!?!
Can anyone explain the Midi today?
I can't stop thinking that the better name for today's puzzle should have been "Dadvertising."
Grateful for a wonderful solve from an experienced constructor. Happy Easter and Zissa Pesach to those celebrating

This was an excellent puzzle! An appropriate level of difficulty for a Sunday, and a super-fun theme. The pun-lovers are pleased today.

Plenty of fun fill, too. CLEMSON crossing CLEMONS was delightful, and any reference to FUTURAMA automatically puts the constructor in my good graces. I haven't seen DARIA in a very long time, so I'm not sure if it still holds up or if that LIAR nostalgia is tricking me again. I do remember an episode where she goes to get a piercing, and she's looking at a list of the body parts they will pierce for you.

DARIA: You spelled "uvula" wrong.
Guy behind counter: No we didn't.
DARIA: *eyes widen in horror*

I remember a few years ago on easter, we had a themed Sunday Easter egg hunt with several EGGs as rebus squares. I was fully on the lookout for some trickery today!! Alas, a wonderful Sunday theme instead! Happy Easter!
Great puzzle. Imaginative. Thanks for the challenge.
Fun. As an ex-scientist I got a kick out of finding T-test. Now I need to construct a puzzle just to sneak in ANCOVA.
It was a good day. No flyspecking!

As a Londoner living in Ireland my first thought regarding 57 across was 'Ryanair' as there are occasional jokes that the next thing Michael O'Leary will be planning is the no seat Boeing (and charging to use the toilet)
Fun, and actually funny. Enjoyed this one a lot!

Thank you, Mr. Donaldson, for a lively & fun Sunday grid -- that was a lovely construction feat! As the 'badvertising' puns came together, they really gave me a chuckle as I also had to rack my brain on some of those products I haven't seen in a while. 

Happy Easter to all who celebrate today!🐰🐣🐰
A really fun and clever puzzle, where my brain had to do lots of thinking on so many clues to suss out the answer.   Thank you to the constructor for the efforts it took to create this one.

Delightful Sunday morning puzzle! 74A was a facepalm moment- "Oh, not those kind of magazines". Likewise 89A with exits, 121A with bolts, and 46D with lab. I love the twisty clues. Thank you, Professor Donaldson and Go Panthers!

Thank you Agatha Christie for giving me 60D, although I believe Hercule Poirot's DIGESTIF of choice was a creme de menthe.

My contribution to this fine theme: "Our weekly news magazine is now printed every other week."

TIME IS FLEETING
Great job Samuel! Also, thanks for your work with the pups.

Great puzzle. Theme answers made me roll my eyes in the best way. Dadvertising indeed. One thing, though. REIKI might be a discipline, and Japanese, but it doesn't heal anything. It's literal hand-waving.
Eggsellent!!!

Great puzzle! Enjoyed the cluing and all the theme answers. The last to fall for me was the Beavis and Butthead sequel (no idea) crossed with the Philippine marinades (also no idea). With only one letter to fill in, I tried a few (AmOBOS? with mARIA as the sequel...ApOBOS...AlOBOS?) and then lucked out with ADOBOS. Will file that away for future reference! 

Thanks for a clever and engaging puzzle, and yes thanks also for your work with Dogs for Better Lives, placing assistance dogs.

This was one of my favorite puzzles of all time. Exactly my type of punny humor. I've been published once in the NYT as a co-constructor (04-09-24 - one of the best days of my life). Would love to collab with you sometime, sir. Bravo! Are you going to the ACPT next week?
My first Sunday solve with no clues! Started doing NYT puzzles about a year ago and suddenly can do most without help.

Looniest? Loopiest? Doofiest? GOOFIEST. (I surely didn't know Alice.) 
Engaging and fun Sunday puzzle with a good theme. Have a good day.
Wow, can't believe I got through this. I'm gonna go have an apple crumble. (By the way, with all the Apple and iPhone references lately, we could use some parity for Android, Unix and Windows, IMHO)

I needed this. Engrossing enough to provide a real mental break. 

Other than the themers, my favorite clue was "Something well taken." 

Got held up at the end not wanting to let go of "loopiest." I was stuck in a mental eddy, trying to figure out what would work to shorten DIGESTIve because that "p" wasn't doing it. 

Thank you Sam!

Had to refresh 6 times today to get the Midi to show up.

I visualize Maxwell's demon, catching signals and tossing them around according to its own agenda.

Very good puzzle. A clever theme without a rebus. Surprising misdirects. Not too hard but with things to think about.


My favorite clue was "No cause for alarm!"?
I was surprised by Clemson -- not my favorite school. 


Happy Easter to those celebrating. 🐣🐰💐
Happy whatever to those celebrating something else.
Cheer up to those not celebrating anything.


I hope to be celebrating 🎉 in a few hours. If my ⛹️‍♀️  🎮 🐓🐓  are 🍀 and focused!

🏵️
Rich, You may have missed it; I linked to one of your favorite songs earlier today. Here's a link to the post with the link. https://www.nytimes.com/shared/comment/4f6b5r?rsrc=cshare&smid=url-share

Are the crossword puzzles getting easier? This week I've beaten my average time by a large amount every day. I don't think I'm getting any sharper at my age of 88! I've been going through the archives; now back to 2013, and I find them much harder than the ones we are seeing now. Anyone else?
Oh yay, still more brand names.

Absolutely lovely puzzle with a theme that made us squeal with delight each time we pieced a fill together. It came relatively easily and I enjoyed the jokey fills not part of the theme (INN, ID TAG). Did anyone else smile at the proximal USS ENTERPRISE? CLEMSON/CLEMONS cross was wildly satisfying too. 

Our only hangup was the OGEE/STOGIE intersection which we had to brute force with consonants until the music came. Not familiar with either but I hope they rhyme! 

Maybe the last puzzle we get to work on together for a while as my other half prepares for her PhD qualifying exams. NO PAIN NO GAIN seems to be the unfortunate philosophy of the academic system and our leisure time may be spent on softer pursuits than tricksy puzzling.
Thanks that was alot of fun. Paired well with my Ben Webster c.d.

I knew I was going to love this puzzle when I saw the title. "Our planes don't have seats!" had me laughing out loud! Excellent Sunday puzzle! A fun theme, and yet the rest of the fill also felt challenging and fresh.
Had APPLEtRouBLES for the longest time instead of APPLECRUMBLES 🫩 Blaming it on my brain feeling a little foggy this morning. Have a good week everyone!
Surprised I solved this one in well under an hour. Fits my inveterate punster mentality and I'm old enough to remember Zorba the Greek, aka Anthony Quinn.
Didn’t have to nitpick this Sunday for a change.
Ok guys, that was a fun puzzle, but can someone explain EXETER to me?
Can anyone explain the italicized clues in the midi today?  I solved the squares but didn’t get the theme

Been away from puzzling for a couple of months for various reasons, but for no particular reason decided to give this one a whirl.  I see most of the usual suspects are still here saying their usual things. Hi, Everyone. Don't have much to say about this one that hasn't been said already, but I'm glad I picked today to come back, since I got to see the news about Sue Jean.

(The way the comment section is set-up is somehow different and looks a little strange to me.)
Ogee and osteoid were too much for me.
It's hard to fathom people including the infamous Rex blog can't enjoy the humor in this puzzle - the theme clues and answers are hilarious and a real feat of construction.  bravo Sam!

I'm a total sucker for a good or bad pun. These are my favorite types of puzzles! 115-Across sent me back to my 1990s childhood when those jeans were popular, and I laughed aloud at 23-Across. This was a real hoot!
I think it shows that most of the time went into the theme, those entries were great but some rough edges elsewhere. Still, worse ways to fill a Sunday!
Interesting puzzle find in the archive; several of the clues have two answers. https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2016/09/28
Can anyone explain the "TATAS" answer? I tried googling and couldn't figure out what the clue or answer was referring to
The cluing on this puzzle is next level, a real joy to behold and solve. Thank you, Mr Donaldson, keep 'em coming!

oh fun!
very clever theme, and smart cluing ~ 
happy sunday, everyone!
Stuck on one letter off. Thought the law exam alternative was GLE and did not know Erato/Elato lol. Still finished it pretty quickly for me for a Sunday!

If you want to be really puzzled, take a look at this one from March 18, 2014 by David Kwong. I must confess that when I first looked over the completed puzzle on Xword Info, I just could not understand how it worked. Had to read the comments and even then had to ponder a bit. Here you go: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=3/18/2014&g=38&d=A ....
77 Down and Across… well done, nifty play. What a cool puzzle. Had me stumped for a bit (83 down) but had fun with this one.
Had to keep coming back and pondering to get this one finished, and had lots of fun along the way. Nice one, Mr. Donaldson!
Loved this one, Sam! Sending love from the PNW!

Cute puzzle! I loved seeing the big man in the grid — even more that CLEMONS and CLEMSON were so cleverly crossed! And while I flat out (and literally) blanked at the 25A/3D cross, it was [A chair might cover one] that wins for aha moment today. I got to AGEN__ and couldn't unsee "agency", as in a chairperson being the head of one. So. Close! Favorite clues were for SNOWFALL and USURPED, but really there were many subtle yet sharp turns of phrase and adjacencies which together made this grid sing! I only wish the editors had taken the step to change the clue for EJECT, in light of recent events. Besides, there are so many more things that can be cleverly ejected! VCR tapes, or a heckler at a show... Only cheers for the constructor and some of the GOOFIEST themers in a while! Let's serenade him with a clip of one of my favorite Clarence Clemons solos: https://youtu.be/JClbQFKoAeo?si=vJQrmlledhQcjpgC Bravo!
Who else remembers the uproar caused when Alexander Haig said he was in control when Reagan was shot?

@Bob T.
p.s. i LOVE the constructor notes. It's so nice to get insight into how the sausage gets made.

Two of my most hated things - puns and brands - all rolled into one 🤬

And I hated the fill in general, too! A double whammy? Actually, a triple one.

SP,
Unless I missed a development, there is only one MIA.
N.B. One is one too many.

@John Something I learned in a short stay in Montreal, just when I needed one.

@James S.
An aperitif is only drunk before a meal, to stimulate the appetite (as if we need that!). Examples: pastis, vermouth, champagne. A digestif is only drunk after the meal, to aid digestion. Examples: brandy, fortified wine, liqueur. The French do not swap the two and will look alarmed if you do.

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight
Yes, Sam Donaldson. But this puzzle is by Samuel A. Donaldson.

@Isabeau 

Don't get me started on the abomination that is "pierogis". A single Polish dumpling is a "pieróg". Many of them are "pierogi". "Pierogis" is rhe sort of perversion of my native tongue that almost makes me feel physical pain.

Still, you can't expect native English speakers to know how to properly pluralize loan words from hundreds of languages, can you?

So while ADOBOS and pierogis may seem offensively wrong to those who know better, they're not, really - not as loan words in English woth its own grammar.

Quiz time!

Predictably, a hamburger in Polish is called "hamburger". But what is the Polish plural of the word?

1. Hamburgers
2. Hamburgeri 
3. Hamburgery
4. Hamburgere
5. Hamburgerz
6. Hamburgerja
7. Hamburgerlo

I'll be back later to reveal the correct answer. The prize might or might not be a tamale or a panini 😃
@Andrzej I’m going with 3 Hamburgery. Served with an Eastern bloc of cheese and Warsaw pact with fried onions. What do i win?
@Andrzej PS I’d settle for some blinis! 🫣

@Andrzej
So, if multiple pierog is pierogi, then I’m plumping for 2 hamburgeri. Though I prefer a beef burger thank you 😉

@Andrzej
Here we are 6 hours later. I read the comments Newest to here, and still don't see the answer. I also choose #6. Do I win? I'd love a panino.

It's hamburgery (but hot-dogs are hot-dogi) so Petrol wins 🥳

I'm feeling gracious so  you're all invited for a burger (possibly a vegan one) if you ever visit Warsaw.
Pierogi in Warsaw; pierogies in Pittsburgh.

@SonOfSJ
I’m so glad to hear SueJean is well, if not quite as sprightly as she used to be. Having lived near to Harrogate in my youth I have fond memories of the beautiful spa town, but of course she must relish being near to her loved ones. Sending her love from ‘Gods own country’. Having lived with Yorkshire folk for so long I hope that gives her a chuckle.

@SonOfSJ Please give your mother my best wishes. I have fond memories of her posts here and have missed seeing them, but I am happy to hear that she is still solving.  Her posts were always interesting and kind. 

I have a connection to Yorkshire through my husband and fond memories of beautiful Harrogate. I’d always hoped to take your mother up on her suggestion that we meet the next time I returned but, unfortunately, I haven’t been back.  I’ll raise a toast to her and your family tonight.

@SonOfSJ

Thanks for sharing the information about your mother.  She was a valued member of this community. Please wish her well.

SonOfSJ,
Please give her my best wishes.

I enjoyed your mother's posts, and always looked for them. Best wishes to her. I have a daughter who lives in Yorkshire and is employed in Harrogate.

Oh, thank you for that information. Suejean was always my best friend on this forum and I've missed her very much. Please let her know how much I've been thinking about her. 

Still hope she might get back here occasionally.

(I dreamt of attending a quilt show in Harrowgate and meeting her in person...but that remained only a dream..)  My husband and I are now living in a "continuing care retirement center," which necessitated a big move--but not as big as her brave journey!  Please give her my warmest regards and a hug.

Your mother has been one of my favorite commentators.

@Vaer: wow, we’ve been wondering about you too!  Nice to see you.

@SonOfSJ  Even if your mother is no longer contributing comments, I hope she sees this roll of supportive cheers. Sometimes people disappear and we're left to wonder what happened to them.  Thank you for telling us where and how she is.  (Hello, Sue Jean!)

I’ve just finished it and this definitely has to be in on of my favorites for connections to my personal life alone, with one being that we are getting married at a chapel named after St Francis de Assisi this summer!

@AT
This reminds me of when my family moved from New Jersey to the Midwest. Whenever we went back to NJ for a visit, we were tasked in bringing back certain items, usually tomatoes and pork roll.

@Katie
The great New Yorker columnist Calvin Trillin has a theory that, at any moment, there are thousands of packages of hometown foods being ferried back and forth across the continent from visiting relatives to longing expatriates. As one who has begged -- yes, begged -- for sunflower seeds and Gunn's gingerbread men to be transported from my ancestral home, I am part of his confirmatory data. (I can no longer get anyone to bring smoked goldeye eastward. 🙇‍♀️ )

p.s. – Two answer-related standouts.  First, that gorgeous answer INPINGE.  And second, CLEMONS and CLEMSON – what are the chances these almost-dupe anagrams would appear in the same box, much less cross?  Mwah!
Alas, the Big Man attended Maryland State, and played football there.

@So Hyun

It goes back to the days when TVs had actual dials. The host of a TV show might say that when a commercial break was about to come on. Nowadays, the equivalent is just “Don’t go anywhere” or “Don’t change the channel.”

@So Hyun 
“Don’t touch that dial, we’ll be back in a minute with more …” was something we would often hear on TV before the program was interrupted by commercials. This was, of course, back when we had to stand up and walk to the set to twist a dial and change channels. I’ll bet the phrase is obscure even to many younger Americans.

Barry Ancona,
Many will not have even had a “dial-up” modem, which of course only emulated the dial. My mother kept her black rotary-dial wall phone, even had the phone company install back-compatible tech to keep it functional, and one of her great-grandsons got to see it in action in the late 2010s.

@Xword Junkie

In my younger years, I bowled in two leagues, and often competed in amateur tournaments, and even once wrote an article for a national sports magazine about bowling with one of my IDOLS, Dick Weber, so I can’t tell you how often I saw AMF on large signs but until now I had no idea what AMF stood for.
The (former) AMF Building (of the late AMF), 261 Madison Avenue, where my father worked for quite a few years. https://i.pinimg.com/originals/f0/b7/d5/f0b7d50dc9be9d95bb1fc28cd057d438.jpg

@Xword Junkie
AMF also owned Harley-Davidson in the '70s, rescuing them from bankruptcy. The quality of those bikes was...not great.
